coredata.dc:title: Flame process constructing CQDs/TiO2-C heterostructure with novel electron transfer channel between internal and external carbon species
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Combustion Institute. Published by Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Combustion Institute. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Bi, Wei
Hu, Yanjie
Jiang, Hao
Lei, Jing
Wan, Xinyi
Zhang, Ling
Li, Chunzhong
coredata.dc:description: The controllable preparation and rational utilization of carbon species produced in flame process is a meaningful research direction. Herein, a CQDs/TiO2 C heterostructure coupling with multi-carbon species was developed in flame process. In the millisecond-level reaction process, part of the residual carbon formed by the incomplete combustion of ethanol penetrates the lattice of TiO2, forming interstitial C (Ci) and substituent C (Cs), and the remaining amorphous carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are adsorbed on the surface of TiO2. In-situ temperature-programmed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (In-situ TPXPS) combined with various characterizations proved that the CQDs/TiO2 C heterostructure was successfully constructed in the flame process (the atomic ratio of the two C species was close to 1:1). The carbon species lead to the reduction of the band gap of the targeted material from 2.8 eV to 2.73 eV, meanwhile, the reduction ability is increased by 21.3%. The synergistic effect enhances the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons by 7 times, achieving a conversion efficiency of 46.21 µmol g −1 h −1 and nearly 100% CO2-to-CO selectivity. Furthermore, the systematic theoretical calculation results visualize this kind of novel electron transfer channel from C to O to CQDs (CO-CQDs).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flame process
Multi-carbon species coupling
Synergistic effect
Novel electron transfer channel
Photoreduction of CO2

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21978088 , 91534202 , 51673063 ), Shanghai Technology Research Leader ( 20XD1433600 ), the Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutes of High Learning, the Basic Research Program of Shanghai (17JC1402300), the Social Development Program of Shanghai (17DZ1200900), the Shanghai City Board of education research and innovation project, and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 222201718002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) In-situ preparation process and formation mechanism of multi-carbon species composite TiO2 in flame field; (b) Post-treatment of f-TiO2 sample to prepare CQDs loaded and C-doping TiO2 (CQDs/TiO2 C), single C-doping TiO2 (TiO2 C) and pure TiO2; (c) in-situ TPXPS for C 1 s spectra of the original sample as-prepared by FSP. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The TEM images, HR-TEM images and the locally enlarged HR-TEM images of sample (a-c) CQDs/TiO2 C, (d-f) TiO2 C and (g-i) pure TiO2, respectively.

(a) The XRD patterns of CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2, respectively. The locally enlarged XRD patterns of the main peak for (b) anatase and (c) rutile TiO2. (d) The Raman spectra of CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2, respectively. The locally enlarged Raman spectra of the (e) main peak of TiO2 and (f) D/G band area of carbon species.

(a) The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra and the corresponding inset of Tauc plots. (b) The Mott-Schottky curves. (c) The schematic diagram of band structure inferred from electrochemical test results. (d) The transient photocurrent responses. (e) The photoluminescence spectra and (f) time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy with 350 nm laser excitation for CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Comparison of the gas products conversion over 3 h for CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2. (b) The conversion rate histogram for CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2. (c) The control group experiment to eliminate the effects of systematic errors or irrelevant variables and (d) the cycle activity test of photocatalytic CO2 reduction process over the optimal sample CQDs/TiO2 C. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

In-situ DRIFT spectra for (a) CQDs/TiO2 C, (b) TiO2 C and (c) pure TiO2 samples during the adsorption stage (the lower part of the orange curve) and the reaction process with light (the upper part of the orange curve). (d) Schematic diagram of the main possible reaction pathways for CQDs/TiO2 C samples.

Calculation model of (a) CQDs/TiO2 C, (b) TiO2 C and (c) pure TiO2. The blue ball is Ti; the red ball is O; the brown ball is C. Band length model of (d) interstitial C and (e) substituent C, and (f) the histogram for doping C band length of different chemical environment. DOS for (g) pure TiO2, (h) TiO2 C, (i) CQDs/TiO2 C. ELF of TiO2 C (j) that the profile is across the substituent C, and (k) the profile is across the interstitial C. ELF of CQDs/TiO2 C (l) that the profile is across the substituent C, and (m) the profile is across the interstitial C. Charge density difference of (o) TiO2 C and (p) CQDs/TiO2 C. Electrons accumulation is in yellow and depletion in blue, respectively. The isosurfaces are set to 0.003 eV Å −3. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Flame process constructing CQDs/TiO2-C heterostructure with novel electron transfer channel between internal and external carbon species

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Hierarchical Nanomaterials, Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science & Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Hierarchical Nanomaterials
Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering
East China University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Hierarchical Nanomaterials, Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science & Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The controllable preparation and rational utilization of carbon species produced in flame process is a meaningful research direction. Herein, a CQDs/TiO2 C heterostructure coupling with multi-carbon species was developed in flame process. In the millisecond-level reaction process, part of the residual carbon formed by the incomplete combustion of ethanol penetrates the lattice of TiO2, forming interstitial C (Ci) and substituent C (Cs), and the remaining amorphous carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are adsorbed on the surface of TiO2. In-situ temperature-programmed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (In-situ TPXPS) combined with various characterizations proved that the CQDs/TiO2 C heterostructure was successfully constructed in the flame process (the atomic ratio of the two C species was close to 1:1). The carbon species lead to the reduction of the band gap of the targeted material from 2.8 eV to 2.73 eV, meanwhile, the reduction ability is increased by 21.3%. The synergistic effect enhances the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons by 7 times, achieving a conversion efficiency of 46.21 µmol g −1 h −1 and nearly 100% CO2-to-CO selectivity. Furthermore, the systematic theoretical calculation results visualize this kind of novel electron transfer channel from C to O to CQDs (CO-CQDs).

ce:section.ce:para: As a by-product of incomplete fuel combustion, carbon nanoparticles (commonly known as soot) formed in the flame field has attracted widespread attention from researchers all over the world [1–5]. So far, scientists have systematically and comprehensively studied the morphology of soot produced by different raw materials [6], particle size distribution of soot [7], mobility size and mass of nascent soot particles [8], internal and surface oxidation of soot agglomerates [9], and the dynamic evolution process of particle impact and adhesion behavior [10]. With the advancement of technology and people's unremitting pursuit of a healthy life, it is still a very important topic to study the formation process of soot and how to control the formation of soot. On the other hand, the controllable preparation and rational utilization of carbon species produced during the combustion process, that is, “turning waste into treasure” is also a significant research direction.

Sirignano et al. [11] prepared a variety of carbon nanoparticles from 1–100 nm by one-step flame method, which exhibited typical fluorescence characteristics from yellow to blue. Russo et al. [12] also reported a controllable preparation strategy for blue and green luminescent carbon nanodots in a controllable fuel-rich flame reactor, and demonstrated the potential of these carbon dots in biomedicine applications. In addition to the purposeful preparation of carbon nanoparticles, the carbon species produced during the combustion process often have an important direct or indirect impact on the performance of the target material. Cho et al. [13] reported a W and C co-doped TiO2 nanowire prepared by flame annealing treatment for the photoelectrochemical water splitting reaction. This strategy doubled the saturation photocurrent of the targeted material, as well as the conductivity and activity are greatly improved. In addition, Meng et al. [14] reported a simple flame deposition method of carbon coating on nickel foam and a study on derived carbon foam with high capacitance performance. The results showed that heat treatment resulted in higher conductivity, more defects, thinner and more corrugated structure of carbon nanosheets, achieving a significant specific capacitance of 172 F g −1 at 2A g −1. In addition to the direct effect of residual carbon, many cases of indirect use of C-to-CO to prepare oxygen vacancies have also been reported. For example, Cho et al. [15] reported a rapid and controllable flame reduction preparation of TiO2 nanowires. In this process, the reducing atmosphere formed by the incomplete combustion of propylene was used to rapidly produce oxygen vacancies on the surface of various oxides, which shows a greatly improved capacity of solar water splitting. In addition, there are many cases of flame-assisted C coating [16–20], which show that the carbon species formed in the flame has a very significant effect on performance. Therefore, how to effectively use the carbon nanoparticles generated in the flame field, and construct a reasonable coupling with the target material to achieve the desired synergistic effect is worthy of in-depth exploration.

Herein, we adopted an efficient functional nano-material synthesis technology, flame spray pyrolysis (FSP), in-situ designed a nano-TiO2 composite material modified by internal and external carbon species. And reveals the effect of a new type of electron transfer channel constructed between lattice C and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the catalytic behavior of the CQDs/TiO2 C system during the CO2 photoreduction process. The study found that the synergy of internal and external carbon species greatly increased the photoreduction performance of the optimized CQDs/TiO2 C sample to 46.21 µmol g −1 h −1, and nearly 100% CO2-to-CO photocatalytic activity. Characterization methods such as in-situ temperature-programmed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (in-situ TPXPS) revealed the objective existence of C species including amorphous loaded CQDs and two kinds of lattice C (interstitial C and substituent C), and provided direct evidence for the construction of the electron transfer channel structure of CO-CQDs. Meanwhile, it was proved that the channel could greatly promote the separation of electron-hole pairs and the generation of effective photo-generated electrons. Furtherly, the in-depth density functional theory (DFT) calculation results provided a theoretical basis and visual structure for the objective existence of this electron transfer channel. What is more, in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (in-situ DRIFTs) also proved that the catalytic mechanism of the targeted catalysts conforms to the “seesaw rule”. The intermediates HCOO− and HCO3 − located at both ends of the seesaw dominate the choice of the targeted material for the catalytic pathway. In short, this work proposes a new concept of electron transfer channel, which provides a new sight for the gas phase synthesis of TiO2 nanocomposites modified by multi-carbon species.

Multi-carbon species modified TiO2 (CQDs/TiO2 C) is in-situ prepared by a standard flame spray pyrolysis device. The schematic diagram of the experiment is shown in Fig. 1 . For details, please refer to [21]. First, a certain amount of tetrabutyl titanate (TBT, Adamas) was dissolved in absolute ethanol, stirred for 5 min and then ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min to form a uniform and stable Ti precursor solution. During the reaction, the precursor solution was controlled at a feed rate of 5 mL min−1. The droplets were atomized by diffused oxygen (diffusion pressure: 0.15 MPa) when reached the nozzle of the capillary. The atomized precursor tiny droplets were burnt in the H2/O2 (3.3 L min−1/16.7 L min−1) diffusion flame. The powder is completely collected on the glass fiber filter by a vacuum collecting device. This as-prepared sample was noted as f-TiO2.

Three sets of targeted samples (CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and TiO2) were obtained by f-TiO2 through different post-processing techniques. A 400 mg sample was placed in a tube furnace. Firstly, pure O2 was introduced and the temperature was raised to 450 °C at 10 °C min−1. Then, it was switched to a pure Ar atmosphere and the temperature was continued to rise to 800 °C, and then annealing for 30 min. The sample obtained is TiO2 modified with lattice C, interstitial C (Ci) and substituent C (Cs), named TiO2 C. In addition, the sample obtained by passing Ar in the whole process is TiO2 modified by CQDs and lattice C, named CQDs/TiO2 C. Finally, the sample obtained by passing O2 in the whole process is pure TiO2, named TiO2.

The morphology and nanoparticle distribution of CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2 were obtained by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100). The CQDs and the lattice distortion was directly observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, TF20/2100F). The lattice structure and the peak shift were tested by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) with the conventional Cu-Kα (1.5406 Å) filament. The influence of optical properties is analyzed by Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM HR Evolution) to determine whether there is residual carbon on the surface of the sample. Besides, the band gap was characterized by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS, U-3900H Spectrophotometer) from 200 nm to 800 nm. The in-situ temperature-programmed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (in-situ TPXPS) was obtained on the spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, ThermoFischer) equipped with a heating and temperature control system. The in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (in-situ DRIFTs) was analyzed with the spectrometer (Bruker TENSOR II) equipped with a vacuum system.

The electrochemical measurement was performed in a conventional three-electrode cell on a typical electrochemical workstation (Zahner PP211). In the photocurrent measurement, a Pt wire electrode was used as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) acted as the reference electrode. The working electrodes were prepared by using the as-prepared samples covered on the surface with Indium tin oxide (ITO) conductor glass, and the effective working area is 1 * 1 cm2. The quartz cell measurement system was filled with 0.5 M Na2SO4 (pH = 6.8) electrolyte for the photocurrent testing, and the Mott-Schottky curves measurement.

Evaluation of CO2 photoreduction performance of samples based on the all-glass automatic online trace gas analysis system (Labsolar 6A, Beijing Perfectlight, China) equipped with a 300 W Xe lamp source (PLS-SXE300D, Beijing Perfectlight, China). Besides, the most critical output system for catalytic performance evaluation, an online gas chromatograph system (GC 2060, Shanghai Ruimin) equipped with two detectors: a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame-ionized detector (FID) connected to a methanation oven. Before the test, 10 mg of the catalyst was dispersed in 0.5 mL of deionized water. After sonication for 10 min, it was uniformly dispersed in the center of a glass fiber filter (Φ = 47 mm), and then transferred to an oven at 60 °C for 30 min. The treated as-prepared catalyst was placed in a quartz reactor with a volume of 400 mL together with 300 uL of deionized water, and repeatedly wash with CO2 gas (99.99%, Shangnong Gas, China) for 2 times. The test procedure began after the equilibrium was reached. Data was measured each hour during the reaction for a total of 3 h. The yield of gas products (CO, CH4 etc.) were determined and calibrated based on standard curves.

The present first principle DFT calculations are performed with the projector augmented wave (PAW) method [22–23]. The exchange-functional is treated using the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional. The cut-off energy of the plane-wave basis is set at 500 eV for optimize calculations of atoms and cell optimization. The vacuum spacing in a direction perpendicular to the plane of the catalyst is at least 12 Å. The Brillouin zone integration is performed using 4 × 4 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-point sampling for a primitive cell [24]. The self-consistent calculations apply a convergence energy threshold of 10−5 eV. The equilibrium lattice constants are optimized with maximum stress on each atom within 0.05 eV Å−1. The Hubbard U (DFT+U) corrections for 3d transition metal by setting according to the literature [25]. And the Ti has been set as 3.5 eV.

Figure 1a shows the process and formation mechanism of in-situ preparation of multi-carbon species composite TiO2 in the flame field. As shown in Fig. 1a, the TBT precursor will undergo two processes of hydrolysis and decomposition rapidly, followed by oxidation to form primary or secondary TiO2 particles [26]. Meanwhile, a part of soot from the thermal decomposition of ethanol as the carbon source to enter the TiO2 crystal lattice, and others remain loaded on the surface in an amorphous form. Eventually, the particles passed by the flame field will be TiO2 composited with multi-carbon species. After the post-treatment process as shown in Fig. 1b, three sets of samples under ideal conditions are obtained, which are noted as CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2, respectively. The specific crystal structure is shown in Fig. 1b. In order to determine the rationality of the treatment process and the type of carbon species in the target product, the in-situ TPXPS characterizations are done and shown in Fig. 1c. Intuitively, the main peak near the binding energy of ~284.6 eV belongs to the amorphous CQDs, and the peaks on both sides of the main peak belong to lattice C. Specifically, the minor peak near the binding energy of ~282.0 eV is attributed to the substituent C-doping (C-Ti), [27] and the small peak near the binding energy of ~ 288.4 eV is attributed to interstitial C-doping (CO-Ti). [28] Since the standard peak position of C used for calibration coincides with the peak of CQDs, it is hard to be visually analyzed. However, it can be inferred from the XPS results in Fig. S1 that the samples after a long period of O2 atmosphere calcination will still have a strong main peak, which will be attributed to the inevitable residual carbon caused by the systematic error of the instrument. Therefore, it can be inferred from the in-situ TPXPS for C 1 s spectra results that the CQDs loaded on the surface of the sample will be preferentially removed at 450 °C. However, the complete removal of lattice C needs to reach up to 800 °C. In addition, the Ti 2p spectra in Figs. S2a and S2b show that the as-prepared fresh TiO2 by FSP has very low crystallinity, and Ti3+ in 460.2 eV accounts for a high proportion [29]. It may be attributed to the fact that the particles stay in the flame field for only a few milliseconds, which cause the inability to crystallize completely. With the increase of temperature, the crystallinity of f-TiO2 changes obviously that the Ti4+ 2p3/2 in 458.8 eV and Ti4+ 2p1/2 in 464.6 eV gradually become the main peaks. Compared with Ti 2p, O 1 s spectra in Fig. S2c, the full spectra in Fig. S2d shows little change.

Figure 2 a-c are TEM images of the targeted CQDs/TiO2 C. Figure 2a shows that nanoparticles are generally less than 50 nm, and the particle size distribution is relatively uniform, which is comparable to the TiO2 particle structure reported in our previous work [30]. Figure 2b is the HR-TEM of the CQDs/TiO2 C sample. Even with such a high contrast of the oxide, the uniform distribution of CQDs on the TiO2 surface can be observed, which is omitted by the yellow coil. The diameter of carbon dots is generally less than 3 nm. In order to analyze the influence of lattice C, part of the area in Fig. 2b has been enlarged to obtain Fig. 2c. The measured lattice spacing is 0.3429 nm, which is significantly smaller than the (101) lattice spacing of standard anatase TiO2. Such a change is due to the lattice shrinkage caused by the doping of lattice C [31–33]. Therefore, Fig. 2a-c are intuitive evidences to prove the co-existence of CQDs and lattice C in the multi-species C composite TiO2 nanoparticles. Fig. 2d-f are TEM images of TiO2 C samples. The morphology of the nanoparticles in Fig. 2d is basically the same as that in Fig. 2a. The biggest difference of this sample is that no obvious CQDs are observed in Fig. 2e. However, Fig. 2f shows that the lattice spacing maintains at 0.3424 nm, indicating that the internal lattice of TiO2 is still doped with C. In brief, Fig. 2d-f illustrate that the post-treatment process can effectively remove CQDs while retaining the lattice C species. Fig. 2g-i are TEM images of pure TiO2 C samples. No obvious CQDs are observed in Fig. 2g and 2h. At the same time, the lattice spacing of Fig. 2i also returns to the standard 0.3498 nm, indicating that the internal and external carbon species in the sample are successfully removed. In addition to such a significant change in the lattice spacing of the (101) crystal plane of the anatase TiO2 shown in Fig. 2, the same morphological characteristics and trends are also observed on the surface of the rutile TiO2 nanoparticles shown in Fig. S3.

In addition to the intuitive evidence obtained from the above electron microscope, some other methods are carried out to further prove the successful formation of the targeted structure. Fig. 3 a is the XRD pattern of CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2, respectively. The overall peak structure is similar. However, by partially magnifying the main peak of the anatase (101) crystal plane at 25.35° shown in Fig. 3b, it can be seen that the CQDs/TiO2 C and TiO2 C samples do have a 0.02° shift compared to pure TiO2. The larger angle indicates that the lattice spacing has become smaller, which is consistent with Fig. 2c and 2f. Similarly, for the main peak of the rutile (110) crystal plane at 27.46°, which is partially enlarged, shown in Fig. 3c, the same trend and results as Fig. 3b are also obtained. In addition, Raman spectroscopy is also one of the commonly used methods to qualitatively characterize the C component. Fig. 3d shows the Raman spectra of CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2, respectively. Obvious TiO2 characteristic peaks can be observed in the figures. Fig. 3e is obtained by partially magnifying the strongest peak. The slight shift in the figure also shows that the inner and outer carbon species have an influence on the Raman scattering effect of TiO2. And the phenomenon of deviation in the same direction conforms to the above characteristics of these three sets of samples. In order to intuitively reflect the change of C species, the 1000–1900 cm−1 show that there is a D band at 1350 cm−1 and G band at 1594 cm−1 [34] in the CQDs/TiO2 C sample without obvious boundaries. While both TiO2 C and pure TiO2 have no signal. On the one hand, this result indicates that CQDs species only exist in CQDs/TiO2 C, on the other hand, it shows that CQDs species are likely to exist in an amorphous state. In order to further quantify the C species in the target sample, elemental analysis (EA) shows the C content in CQDs/TiO2 C is 0.04%; the C content in TiO2 C is 0.02%, and the C content in pure TiO2 is 0. By comparing CQDs/TiO2 C and TiO2 C, it is inferred that the ratio of CQDs to lattice C is around 1:1, and the statistical data are listed in Table S1. In addition, finding out the source of C species is also critical. By replacing the precursor with titanium tetrachloride, we verified whether C is derived from the organic component in tetrabutyl titanate or the ethanol solvent. The Raman results in Fig. S4 show that there are obvious C peaks (D band and G band) with the titanium tetrachloride precursor, indicating that the C species is mainly derived from ethanol solvent.

In order to explore the optical and photoelectrochemical properties of the targeted materials, we have combined spectral analysis and electrochemical testing methods to discuss the band structure of the material in detail. The result of the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra in Fig. 4 a shows that the C species does not significantly improve the absorption of the visible light region, but the corresponding inset of Tauc plots shows that the C species still has a certain change in the band gap. The band gap of CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2 are 2.73 eV, 2.77 eV and 2.80 eV, respectively. The Mott-Schottky curves in Fig. 4b show that the targeted materials all exhibit the characteristics of n-type semiconductors [35]. And the flat band potentials obtained after tangent processing are −0.512 V, −0.413 V and −0.387 V vasus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) For CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2. The obtained band structures of the three targeted materials are shown in Fig. 4c. The results show that their conduction band potentials (ECB) exceed the theoretical minimum potential of CO2/CO (0.53 eV), which reveals the possibility of the thermodynamics of producing CO. Notably, CQDs/TiO2 C has the lowest band gap of 2.73 eV, but the most negative ECB of −0.712 eV, which indicates that TiO2 modified by multi-carbon species has easier separation of electron-hole pairs and stronger reduction ability. In contrast, the band gap of TiO2 C is wider, and the ECB shifts to −0.613 eV in the positive direction, which means its reduction ability is reduced. On the other hand, the pure TiO2 has the widest band gap of 2.80 eV and the most positive ECB of −0.587 eV, so its electron-hole separation possibility is the lowest, with the worst reduction ability. Besides, the same results can be drawn from the photocurrent test in Fig. 4d. The results show that CQDs/TiO2 C has the strongest photocurrent response, followed by TiO2 C, and pure TiO2 has the lowest photocurrent intensity, which indicates that internal and external carbon species can greatly improve the mobility of electrons from the inside to the surface. In addition, in order to obtain the degree of recombination of photogenerated electrons and photogenerated holes, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra result in Fig. 4e shows that the order of fluorescence intensity is pure TiO2>TiO2
C>CQDs/TiO2 C. This indicates that the order of the recombination probability of photogenerated electrons and photogenerated holes is pure TiO2>TiO2
C>CQDs/TiO2 C, which is inversely proportional to the number of electrons effectively used for photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Directly, the time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy results in Fig. 4f show that the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs/TiO2 C is 7.818 ns, followed by TiO2 C with 7.394 ns, and pure TiO2 with the shortest lifetime of 7.070 ns. In brief, the synergy between the external CQDs species and the internal lattice C species can not only optimize the band structure, but also act as a “bridge” to greatly improve the electron transfer efficiency, as well as extend the lifetime of electrons participating in photocatalytic reactions.

Figure 5 shows the catalytic performance of the targeted materials. The results in Fig. 5a show that the CO2-to-CO photoreduction performance of the optimal CQDs/TiO2 C sample reaches 135.86 µmol g −1 over 3 h, which is 25.4% higher than that of TiO2 C and even 61.5% better than pure TiO2. The reason for the obvious performance improvement is the rapid electron migration channel constructed between the internal and external carbon species, which greatly improves the utilization of photogenerated electrons. A more intuitive comparison of the catalytic efficiency of the targeted materials is shown in the conversion rate histogram in Fig. 5b. The conversion rates of CO for CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2 are 46.21 µmol g −1 h −1, 33.86 µmol g −1 h −1, and 18.02 µmol g −1 h −1, respectively. However, almost no CH4 signal is detected. The results show that the targeted materials have extremely high selectivity to the product CO. To further prove our view, 4 sets of controlled trials (without photocatalyst, without light, without water and changing the atmosphere to Ar) in Fig. 5c, prove that the good sealing of the instrument, indicating the reliability of the test results. In addition, the cycle activity test results in Fig. 5d show that the performance of the CQDs/TiO2 C sample has almost no degradation in three cycles, proving the excellent stability of the catalyst. Besides, the cycle activity test over TiO2 C and pure TiO2 are shown in Fig. S5.

In order to clarify the reaction mechanism, the results of the in-situ DRIFTs for CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2 samples are shown in Fig. 6 . Figure 6a discusses the test results of the CQDs/TiO2 C sample in the adsorption stage (the lower part of the orange curve) and the reaction process with light (the upper part of the orange curve). At the beginning of the adsorption stage, an obvious H2O peak appears at 1640 cm−1 [36]. With the accumulation of time, the H2O peak begin to increase and reaches adsorption equilibrium after 60 min. Meanwhile, some faint peaks begin to appear, such as formate (HCOO−, 1549 cm−1, 1384 cm−1), carbonate (CO3 2−, 1631 cm−1, 1313 cm−1), bicarbonate or CO2 − (HCO3 − / CO2 −, 1659 cm−1, 1208 cm−1), and characteristic carbonate/formate groups (1178 cm−1, 1126 cm−1) [37–41]. When entering the reaction process with light, the peak intensity of H2O drops significantly, because H2O participates in the reaction process and be consumed. However, all the other peaks mentioned above have been significantly enhanced, and the accumulation of intermediate substances indicates that light has promoted the kinetic process of CO2 reduction; accelerated the generation of electrons and participated in the process of chemical reactions. In addition, for the CQDs/TiO2 C sample, there are three more obvious HCOO− peaks (1776 cm−1, 1331 cm−1, 1275 cm−1) [37,42] appear in the spectrum. What is more, a new obvious CO2 − adsorption peak at 1247 cm−1 appears [39], which is the key step for electrons to participate in the reaction. It further shows that light will accelerate the overflow of electrons into the reactant CO2 and complete the CO2-to-CO photoreduction process. In addition, a prominent step peak appears at 1495 cm−1, which is attributed to the strong adsorption of CO on the surface of the material. To a certain extent, this reflects the reason why CO is a characteristic product. The general change trend of the TiO2 C sample in Fig. 6b from the adsorption stage to the light stage is consistent with that of CQDs/TiO2 C, but the biggest difference is that a strong HCOO− absorption peak at 1384 cm−1 disappears, as well as the other HCOO− absorption peaks are also generally weakened. In addition, the b-HCO3 − peak at 1208 cm−1 is greatly enhanced, indicating that TiO2 C is more likely to form HCO3 − intermediates to complete the CO2-to-CO conversion process. However, our previous work [30] reveals the efficiency of CO production in such a process is not as good as the process of forming HCOO− intermediate, therefore, the apparent CO adsorption peak at 1495 cm−1 is relatively weakened. The peak pattern of pure TiO2 in Fig. 6c is significantly different than the other two catalysts. Firstly, there is only one signal peak of HCOO− at 1331 cm−1, but the strong peaks of CO3 2− and HCO3 − are basically retained, which means that the proportion of intermediate HCOO− in the catalytic process is reduced, while the proportion of CO3 2− and HCO3 − have increased. This may be the essential cause of the worst performance. Notably, there are two obvious inverted peaks in the carbonate and formate groups area of 1100–1200 cm−1, which are caused by the consumption of intermediates produced in the adsorption stage. The inability to effectively accumulate carbonate and formate may be one of the reasons for the reduced performance. Figure 6d shows the main possible reactions pathways for CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2 based on the above in-situ DRIFTs results. The results show that two reaction pathways will exist in the reaction system at the same time. The presence and type of carbon species will directly affect the proportion of reaction pathways, which can be visually illustrated as the “seesaw rule”. One end of the seesaw is the intermediate HCOO−, and the other end is the intermediate HCO3 −. When there are internal and external carbon species in the catalyst, the HCOO− pathway dominates, and when no carbon species exists, the HCO3 − pathway dominates.

The in-situ DRIFTs have clarified the potential reaction process and mechanism. In order to further explore the structure-activity relationship, DFT simulation calculations are used to verify the previous conjectures derived from experiments. Figure 7 a-c correspond to the CQDs/TiO2 C, TiO2 C and pure TiO2 models respectively. Combining the experimental results of the previous XRD, in-situ TPXPS and Raman spectroscopy, the highly exposed anatase (101) surface will be used as the main modeling object (The blue balls in the model are Ti atoms, red are O atoms, and brown are C atoms). In Fig. 7c, amorphous CQDs are loaded on the surface, and the internal lattice C is divided into interstitial C (Ci) and substituent C (Cs). Figure 7d and 7e separately model the chemical environment of these two different types of lattice C and calculate the bond lengths. Figure 7f counts the calculation results of the bond lengths of 10 chemical bonds that may bond with C. The results show that Ci tends to bond with O, while Cs tends to bond with Ti, which further prove the rationality of the C 1 s peak deconvolution results in XPS from the perspective of simulation calculation. The CO-Ti bond in the XPS results refers to the interstitial C1 here; on the other hand, the Ti-C bond refers to the lattice C2 that substitute the O atoms. Figure 7g-i show the calculated density of states (DOS) of the above three models. The DOS results show that the electron mobility of TiO2 C is higher than that of pure TiO2, while the electron mobility of CQDs/TiO2 C is the highest. With the enrichment of C species, the band gap of the targeted materials gradually narrows, and the ECB of CQDs/TiO2 C gradually approaches the Fermi level, which greatly increases the electronic conductivity. This characteristic is highly consistent with the result of UV–vis DRS. Besides, the results of DOS show that lattice C doping and loading CQDs work together to construct a “bridge” between the inside of the material and the surface, which can highly accelerate the efficiency of electron transfer from the inside lattice to the surface. Figure 7j-k are electron localization function (ELF) of TiO2 C that the profile is across the Cs, and the Ci, respectively. The ELF diagram shows that the lattice C will affect the electron transfer form. Compared with the pure TiO2 in Fig. S6, it can be clearly observed that the electrons in TiO2 C are more active. The electron transfer direction is transferred from the lattice C to the adjacent lattice O. Or continue to transfer to the lattice Ti and then transfer to the O atoms on the surface. Furthermore, after loading CQDs shown in Fig. 7l-m, the electronic localization becomes stronger and the electron transfer becomes more obvious. Surface-loaded CQDs will further induce internal electron enrichment to the surface. The specific transfer pathway is CO-CQDs, or CO-Ti-O-CQDs. More intuitively, the result of the charge density difference in Fig. 7o-7p shows how the electron migrates from the inside to the surface CQDs (the yellow in the figure represents electrons accumulation and blue represents electron depletion). This result is consistent with the ELF result, but more concretely reflects that the main effective electron transfer path is CO-CQDs. In order to clarify the role of Ti atoms in the electron transfer process, the result of charge density difference with an isosurface value of 0.006 eV Å −3 is shown in Fig. S7. The results show that the electron transfer around Ti is not obvious, only a small part of Ti atoms will get small content of electrons from the surrounding O atoms. So, CO-Ti-O-CQDs is also a minor electron transfer channel. Briefly, the existing structure-activity relationship indicates that the synergy of internal and external carbon species will serve as a new type of electron transfer channel to facilitate the efficient gas-solid phase CO2 photoreduction process. In addition, as an extension of this work, some other supplementary materials are shown in Fig. S8-S10 and Table S2.

In this work, we have designed a CQDs/TiO2 C heterostructure coupling with multi-carbon species in the flame process, and further studied the formation process and catalytic mechanism. Growth mechanism studies revealed that in the millisecond-level reaction process, part of the residual carbon formed by incomplete combustion of ethanol will penetrate the TiO2 lattice, forming interstitial C (Ci) and substituent C (Cs), while the remaining amorphous CQDs are adsorbed on the surface of TiO2. The detailed analysis of in-situ TPXPS showed the dynamic change process of the targeted material, indicating that CQDs/TiO2 C heterostructure was successfully constructed in the flame process. Elemental analysis showed that the atomic ratio of lattice C and CQDs is close to 1:1. The addition of carbon species reduced the band gap of the targeted material from 2.8 eV to 2.73 eV, as well as increased the reduction ability by 21.3%. The synergistic effect increases the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons by 7 times, achieving a conversion efficiency of 46.21 µmol g −1 h −1 and a nearly 100% CO2-to-CO selectivity. What is more, the systematic theoretical calculation results provided a visual evidence that this new electron transfer channel is from C to O to CQDs (CO-CQDs).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no known competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21978088, 91534202, 51673063), Shanghai Technology Research Leader (20XD1433600), the Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutes of High Learning, the Basic Research Program of Shanghai (17JC1402300), the Social Development Program of Shanghai (17DZ1200900), the Shanghai City Board of education research and innovation project, and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (222201718002).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.combustflame.2021.01.044.
